question_text: A free-body diagram shows all the forces acting on an object. For an object in equilibrium, what must be true about the arrows representing the forces on its free-body diagram?
correct_answer: The arrows must form a closed shape (or balance out). All forces in one direction must be equal to all forces in the opposite direction. The resultant of all the arrows must be zero, meaning if you add all the force vectors together, they cancel out completely.
explanation: In a free-body diagram for an object in equilibrium, the forces must balance. This means that when you combine all the force arrows, they must add up to give zero resultant force. Visually, if you arrange them tip-to-tail, they form a closed loop. For example, if an object has an upward force of 100 N, there must be a downward force (or forces) that total exactly 100 N.
